completeness.rationale: The triage captured most expected items: manual save trigger ('clicks Save in the toolbar'), CSV import connection, large list size (~200 tasks), crash starting after CSV import, and macOS 14.2. Two minor gaps: the encoding-related error message text was not explicitly extracted (reporter couldn't read it, but the agent didn't probe further), and the exact version is logged as '2.3.x' rather than '2.3.1'.

accuracy.rationale: The triage identifies CSV import as the trigger and encoding as the mechanism, which matches acceptable path 1 exactly. The root cause hypothesis — 'malformed characters, encoding issues, or values that exceed expected field limits' in the 'save/serialization code path' — is well-aligned with the actual cause (non-ASCII characters causing the save serializer to panic). The size-dependency is also noted. No meaningful inaccuracies.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent asked about crash behavior, list size, list specificity, OS/version, unusual content, and attempted to obtain a crash report. The key connections were all surfaced. One obvious follow-up was skipped: the agent never explicitly asked whether auto-save triggers the same crash, which is listed as expected information and would distinguish a toolbar-save-specific code path from a general save issue.

economy.rationale: Three turns, each building directly on the previous response and yielding actionable new information. No redundant questions. The pairing of 'how many tasks' with 'does it happen on other lists' in turn 2, and 'unusual content' with 'crash report' in turn 3, were efficient combinations.

question_quality.rationale: The question about 'unusual content — copy-pasted text, special characters, emoji' in turn 3 was diagnostically sophisticated and directly elicited the CSV import disclosure that the reporter had not volunteered. The turn 2 question probing whether the crash is list-specific (not just 'do you have a large list') showed genuine diagnostic reasoning about isolating the variable.

actionability.rationale: The summary includes a clear root cause hypothesis pointing to the save/serialization code path, specific reproduction steps with environmental details, four concrete recommended fixes (input sanitization at import, encoding normalization, error dialog lifecycle fix, autosave recovery), and a proposed test case with specific character types to try. A developer could begin inspecting the serializer immediately.

notable_strengths: The 'unusual content / special characters' question in turn 3 was the key diagnostic move — it drew out the CSV import detail the reporter had not mentioned, unlocking the real failure mode
The triage summary is exceptionally actionable: reproduction steps, recommended fixes, and a proposed test case are all specific enough for a developer to act on immediately
The agent correctly identified encoding in the serialization path as the mechanism, matching the actual root cause without having crash log data
notable_weaknesses: The auto-save vs. manual save distinction was never explicitly probed — the reporter mentioned clicking Save in the toolbar, but the agent never confirmed whether auto-save triggers the same crash, which is diagnostically important
The exact patch version (2.3.1) was not pinned down; the agent accepted '2.3 something' without a follow-up

The briefly-flashing error dialog text was not pursued further — even asking the reporter to trigger the crash again with a screen recording or slow-motion attempt could have surfaced the 'encoding' keyword
most_insightful_question: Do any of your tasks have unusual content? For example, very long descriptions, copy-pasted text from other apps, file attachments, or special characters like emoji?
missed_opportunities: Ask whether auto-save (if the app has it) crashes as well, or whether the crash is specific to the toolbar Save button — this distinguishes the manual save code path from the auto-save path
Ask the reporter to attempt triggering the crash again and try to screenshot or video the flashing error dialog, since its text would likely contain 'encoding' and confirm the hypothesis

Ask what application the CSV was originally exported from — different sources (Excel, Google Sheets, Numbers) use different encodings and delimiters that could narrow down the character set responsible
